title: ink-flow: How a Skill Grew Into a Product
summary: From a simple blog-publishing Claude Code Skill to a full multi-platform content distribution product. OPC Loop drove 8 iterations, a platforms/ shared knowledge layer emerged, four-layer testing (UT/CT/API/Browser UI) was demanded and delivered, and the real texture of agent-era product incubation became clear.
body:
## Starting Point: A Skill Called /blog

Three weeks ago, I had a dead-simple Skill called `/blog`. It published markdown from Obsidian to my blog. That's it.

But after every blog post, there was a manual tail: copy HTML into WeChat's editor (their `<pre>` doesn't support horizontal scrolling, fun times), post to Zhihu, compose a Twitter thread, write a LinkedIn post. Every platform has different formatting rules, and every time I'd re-discover the same gotchas.

That's where ink-flow started — not from a grand product vision, but from "I'm sick of copy-pasting."

## The Name: Ink Flows

Naming took a dozen attempts. Claude's suggestions were all corporate-safe: content-flow, cross-post-hub, multi-publish. Pure AI slop.

We landed on ink-flow — 墨水流淌 (ink flowing). Thoughts become words, flowing to every channel. The name has imagery, and `ink` hints that the essence of content isn't distribution, it's writing.

## Architecture: The platforms/ Shared Knowledge Layer

When I got serious about multi-platform distribution, the first architecture question was: **where does platform knowledge live?**

WeChat doesn't support `<pre>` horizontal scrolling — you need `white-space: pre-wrap`. Zhihu forbids `<style>` tags. Twitter has 280-char limits. LinkedIn articles and posts are entirely different formats.

This knowledge has two consumers:
1. **CLI adapters** — Python code calling platform APIs
2. **Skill scripts** — lint.py and preview.py inside Claude Code

OPC review produced two options. We picked the recommended one: `platforms/` inside the Python package (`cli/platforms/`), with `make install-skill` copying it to the Skill directory.

```
ink-flow/
├── cli/
│   ├── platforms/          # shared knowledge layer
│   │   ├── wechat/
│   │   │   ├── spec.py      # pure data: field limits, sizes
│   │   │   └── lint_rules.py # pure functions: HTML → violations
│   │   └── zhihu/ ...
│   └── wechat/              # adapters (import platforms.wechat.spec)
├── skill/
│   ├── SKILL.md
│   └── scripts/             # lint.py, preview.py
└── Makefile                 # install-skill target
```

The key design principle: **platforms/ = pure data + pure functions, zero I/O.** spec.py has only constants (`MAX_TITLE_LEN = 64`), lint_rules.py has only pure functions (`check(html) -> list[Finding]`). All I/O and side effects live in the adapter layer.

This clean boundary makes testing trivial — pure functions get direct unit tests, no mocking needed.

## OPC Loop: 8 Automated Iterations

The entire platforms/ migration was driven by OPC Loop. The plan decomposed into 8 units:

1. merge origin/main
2. implement-platforms (create shared layer skeleton)
3. review (OPC multi-role review)
4. fix (address review findings)
5. implement-skill (update Skill distribution)
6. review
7. fix
8. e2e-verify

14 ticks total, surviving two context compactions (session got so long Claude auto-compressed context). After each compaction, `.harness/flow-state.json` carried enough state to resume.

The biggest finding: **OPC Loop performs well in build→review→fix cycles, but tends toward "fake verification" in e2e-verify** — it ran unit tests and declared success, while the installed version's import path was actually broken.

## Four-Layer Testing: Demanded by the User

My testing requirement was explicit in CLAUDE.md: "Tests must cover UT, CT, API Testing, and Browser UI Testing, 100% coverage."

But after OPC Loop completed, I asked Claude: any regrets?

It was honest. Four regrets:
1. lint.py's import path was broken (not updated after migration)
2. No CT (component tests) — lint.py as subprocess wasn't tested
3. No API Testing — preview.py's HTTP endpoints weren't tested
4. No Browser UI Testing — no Playwright tests for the dashboard

I said: Why didn't you deliver this? I explicitly asked for it.

No excuses. Just fixed it. The final test matrix:

| Layer | File | Count | Verifies |
|---|---|---|---|
| UT | `wechat/test_lint_rules.py` + `zhihu/...` | 81 | Pure function correctness |
| CT | `test_lint_py.py` | 15 | lint.py as subprocess |
| API | `test_preview.py` (API classes) | 21 | HTTP endpoint responses |
| Browser UI | `test_preview.py` (Playwright) | 15 | Dashboard visual + interaction |

All 1161 tests pass.

**Key insight: test layer separation is an explicit contract, not a nice-to-have.** If you don't spell out each layer in your acceptance criteria, the agent will gravitate toward the easiest layer (unit tests) and skip the hard ones (browser UI tests).

## The Installed Version Wiring Gap

The most painful lesson: **dev environment tests passing ≠ installed version working.**

lint.py worked perfectly in the dev environment (running from the repo root). But after `make install-skill`, the installed version's `importlib.import_module("platforms.wechat.lint_rules")` threw ModuleNotFoundError.

Root cause: `sys.path.insert(0, str(Path(__file__).parent))` pointed to `scripts/`, but `platforms/` lives under the skill root, not under `scripts/`. Changing to `Path(__file__).parent.parent` fixed it.

Same class of bug as "unit tests passing ≠ feature working," but on a different axis — not a code-level wiring gap, but a **distribution-level wiring gap**. File copying, path rewriting, dependency trimming can all silently break at install time.

After the fix, I added an end-to-end verification: `make install-skill && python3 ~/.claude/skills/ink-flow/scripts/lint.py test-file.html`. This step belongs in CI.

## Preview Dashboard

preview.py is a single-file HTTP server that launches an Apple-style dark theme dashboard at localhost:9877:

- Left sidebar: All Materials + platform channels (WeChat 375px, Zhihu 690px, Twitter Thread, LinkedIn Post, Blog CN, Blog EN)
- Right top: materials/files area
- Right bottom: live preview iframe

Clicking a channel loads the corresponding platform preview in the iframe. WeChat preview is 375px wide (simulating mobile), Zhihu is 690px (desktop), Twitter renders as a thread.

This dashboard survived 3 rounds of OPC review, fixing frosted glass blur, a11y (keyboard navigation + ARIA roles), dark theme surface variables, and more.

## Looking Back: Agent-Era Product Incubation Patterns

From `/blog` to ink-flow took about three weeks across multiple sessions. A few patterns emerged:

**1. A Skill is a product seed.** A SKILL.md file is an MVP spec. When it outgrows itself, CLI, shared layers, tests, and distribution mechanisms grow naturally.

**2. OPC Loop is the iteration engine.** Plan → Build → Review → Fix cycles, each round with independent evaluator roles. 14 ticks completed a full migration, including two reviews, two fixes, and one e2e verify.

**3. Human value is in acceptance criteria.** The agent executes well, but if you don't explicitly demand "four-layer testing," it'll skip browser UI tests. If you don't verify the installed version, it'll assume dev-passing is enough. **You define quality; you don't execute it.**

**4. The 70% philosophy.** Don't chase perfection. The `cli` package should be renamed to `ink_flow` (two OPC agents flagged it), but not in this round. Separate PR, mechanical replacement. Put energy on the highest-leverage thing.
